content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 30, 2016

Marcia L. Scully
General Counsel
Metropolitan Water District of Southern California
Box 54153
Los Angeles, CA 90054-0153

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-121

Dear Ms. Scully:

You are the General Counsel for the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California. This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ on behalf of that organization.

Please note that we are only providing advice under the lobbying provisions of the Act and not under other general lobbying prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. Are the expenses of Metropolitan Water District of California's ("Metropolitan") inspection tours required to be reported on Metropolitan's Lobbyist Employer quarterly report as "Other Payments to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action," when an occasional qualifying reportable state official is a tour guest?

2. Can the total amount of the Activity Expenses for the tours be pro-rated on the report to reflect the total activity costs for only the state qualifying officials present on a tour?

3. If the costs of inspection tours are required to be reported under "Other Payments to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action," can the expenses be pro-rated to reflect only the costs paid for reportable state officials? (For example, if the cost of a tour bus for 40 passengers is $13,000 and there is one qualifying reportable state official on the tour, can the reportable cost for the single qualifying official be calculated on a pro rata basis?)

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-121
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. The expenses of Metropolitan's inspection tours must be reported on Metropolitan's
Lobbyist Employer quarterly reports as "Other Payments to Influence Legislative or
Administrative Action" when a qualifying reportable state official is a tour guest or when
the tours are aimed at influencing state legislative or administrative action.²

2. Yes. The total amount of the Activity Expenses reported can be pro-rated to reflect the total
activity costs for only the state qualifying officials present on a tour.

3. If the costs of inspection tours are required to be reported under "Other Payments to
Influence Legislative or Administrative Action," and the payment for the transportation is
necessary for the tour to take place at all, the reported expenses cannot be pro-rated to
reflect the costs paid for only the reportable state officials. The entire cost of the
transportation for the tour must be reported regardless of the ratio of qualifying officials to
non-qualifying officials.

FACTS³

Metropolitan requests written advice about the reporting requirements for a lobbyist
employer under the Act with regard to the field inspection tours of Metropolitan and its related
facilities when possibly one to three guests out of approximately 40 tour guests on some tours are a
reportable person for lobbying purposes.

Metropolitan is a special district local agency created pursuant to state statute with the
primary purpose of providing a supplemental domestic supply of water for domestic and municipal use at
wholesale rates to its member public agencies. Metropolitan is comprised of 26 member public
agencies, including 14 cities, 11 municipal water districts, and one county water authority.
Metropolitan is governed by a board of directors appointed by its member public agencies. There
are currently 38 directors on Metropolitan's Board of Directors. Metropolitan reports its lobbying
expenses to the Commission as a Lobbyist Employer.

In 1994, the Commission issued an informal advice letter (Bennion Advice Letter No. 1-94-
242) to Metropolitan. Though not noted in the letter, you have stated the letter involved a chartered
bus tour of the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta provided to legislators by Metropolitan for
information purposes. The letter provided that gifts not considered to be informational items of the
trip, such as provided meals, would be reportable activity expenses. Also the expenses directly

² In addition to when a state qualifying official is present, the expenses of Metropolitan's inspection tours
would be required to be reported as other payments to influence legislative or administrative action if they are designed
to influence state legislative or administrative action, such as urging participants on the tours to contact state legislators
to support or oppose particular legislation.

³ These facts are those stated in your incoming request for advice. The Commission is not the finder of fact
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are
complete and accurate.

File No. A-16-121
Page No. 3

related to the bus tour were to be reported by the lobbyist employer under "Other Payments to
Influence Legislative or Administrative Action."

Since this advice was provided, items such as the entire cost of providing the bus for the
informational tour have been included by Metropolitan on Attachment Form 640 of its lobbyist
employer statements under total payments of more than $250 during the calendar quarter for
lobbying activity, and have been itemized on that form. Also, on Form 635, Report of Lobbyist
Employer, under Part C-Activity Expenses, the itemized gift of meals or lodging provided to
reportable officials are itemized, and, under total amount of the activity, the total amount of the
expenses, such as the meals, provided to all tour participants, is listed.

While Metropolitan does continue to provide some inspection tours for legislators, and
follows the Commission's guidelines for reporting the expenses of these tours on its lobbyist
employer reports, most of the tours authorized by Metropolitan's Board of Directors under
Metropolitan's Administrative Code are not directed toward legislators or state administrative
officials.

Section 2610 of Metropolitan's Administrative Code provides that:

"During each fiscal year (July 1 to June 30) each of the District's directors may
sponsor field inspection trips of District and related facilities for the purpose of
providing leading citizens and other interested persons, preferably from the
agency represented by the director, with firsthand knowledge of the District's
operations, as follows: Either (A) one two-day trip or one three-day trip over the
Colorado River Aqueduct System under Section 2611 and one one-day trip under
Section 2613, or (B) three one-day trips over local State Water Project and
District facilities and one one-day trip under Section 2613."

Administrative Code section 2615(a) further explains that:

"The purpose of these inspection trips is to disseminate District information;
provide elected and appointed officials, members of the business and
environmental communities, members of the media, and other leading citizens
and interested persons from each member public agency with visual inspection of
District and related facilities; and acquaint them with issues facing the water
industry and the District's operations, programs, and objectives, both present and
future. Each director shall be responsible for the individual selection of guests, the
choice of inspection trip and the inspection trip dates."

Section 2615(b) states that each director has the obligation to periodically sponsor inspection
trips and subpart (c) provides that:

"Inspection trip guests should be selected from within the area of the public
agency represented by the sponsoring director. Each director should select guests
who occupy positions of leadership in their communities and other interested
persons as described in subsection (a) above."

File No. A-16-121
Page No. 4

Pursuant to these directives, most of the invited tour guests are local members of the
community of the sponsoring director. You state that tours with no qualifying state officials as
guests are not reported on Metropolitan's lobbyist employer reports. However, at times, a tour will
have a small number of qualifying state officials as guests on these trips, possibly one to maybe
three. In these instances, Metropolitan currently itemizes any meals, lodging or other reportable
gifts under the Activity Expenses Part C on Form 635, reports the total cost of the meals, lodging
and other reportable gifts given to the entire tour group under "Total Amount of Activity", reports
the total cost of the bus tour under "Other Payments to Influence" on Forms 635 and 640, and
itemizes all of the expenses for the entire trip over $250 on Form 640. You state that the tours are
not specifically conducted for or directed at Legislators, though some may audit from time to time.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires lobbyist employers to disclose payments made in connection with
influencing legislative and administrative action. (Section 86115.) Among other items, lobbyist
employers must itemize activity expenses and payments which would not have been made except to
support or assist lobbying activity. (Section 86116.) Activity expenses include gifts provided to a
state official or state candidate. (Section 86111.)

Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as:

"(a) "Gift" means, except as provided in subdivision (b), any payment to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to
official status..."

"(b) The term "gift" does not include:

"(1) Informational material such as books, reports, pamphlets, calendars, or
periodicals. No payment for travel or reimbursement for any expenses shall be
deemed "informational material."

Regulation 18942.1 provides:

"Informational material" means any goods or service that serves primarily to
convey information and that is provided to the official for the purpose of assisting
him or her in the performance of his or her official duties or the duties of the
elective office he or she seeks. Informational material may include:

...

"(c) On-site demonstrations, tours, or inspections. Transportation provided to or
in connection with an on-site demonstration, tour, or inspection is also considered
"informational material" when any of the following apply:

File No. A-16-121
Page No. 5

"(1) The transportation serves as the means by which the information is conveyed
and is integral to the conveyance of the information, such as an aerial tour over an
area.

"(2) The transportation is provided solely at the site of a demonstration, tour, or
inspection, including to and from an area of that site that is legally inaccessible to
the public.

"(3) The transportation is to or form a site when there is no reasonable, publicly-available
commercial transportation available to that site and the sportation provided is limited to
the segment for which public transportation is not available."

Thus, the on-site tour and any transportation that is not commercially obtainable and is also
an integral part of the on-site tour is considered informational material.

As noted above, a purpose of these inspection trips is to acquaint elected officials with
issues facing the water industry and the District's operations, programs, and objectives, both present
and future.

Section 86116(h)(1) requires lobbyist employers to report "the total of all other payments to
influence legislative or administrative action . . .."

Regulation 18239(d)(4) further provides that "'influencing legislative or administrative
action' means communicating directly or taking any other action for the principal purpose of
supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing any legislative or
administrative action." (Emphasis added.)

Elected officials are one of the types of individuals targeted for these tours as listed in
Administrative Code section 2615(a). Making legislators aware of issues facing the water industry
and the district's objectives are ways of influencing potential legislation, especially when
legislators are invited on the tours based upon the official positions they hold. Because such
individuals are selected because they are members of the Legislature (i.e. individuals charged with
the responsibility of making laws), tours acquainting them with issues facing the water industry and
with district objectives that in any way attempt to influence legislative or administrative action must
be disclosed under the category "Other Payments to Influence." (Regulation 18616.)

These reported expenses for the bus tour cannot be pro-rated. Section 86116.5(a)(2) states
that "[p]ayments and any other expenses which would not have been incurred but for the filer's
activities to influence or attempt to influence legislative or administrative action" must be reported.
Metropolitan must report the entire amount spent to account for the bus tour as it currently does, because that is the
amount of the expenses incurred as a result of the tour and the tour could not have taken place
without this transportation. Reporting only a portion of the cost of the tour does not reflect an
accurate picture of the amount expended to influence qualifying officials. Therefore, if qualifying
officials are on the bus the entire cost of this item must be reported.

File No. A-16-121
Page No. 6

However, any transportation which is not part of the on-site tour such as, transportation to
and from the origin of the tour, are gifts and reportable activity expenses. (Briggs Advice Letter,
No. A-93-210 and Barker Advice Letter, No. A-93-186.)

In addition, other benefits, such as food and beverages, are gifts and must be disclosed as
activity expenses. Because these tours are by invitation only, the pro rata share of the cost of the
food is all that is required to be reported as an activity expense per qualifying official. (Regulation
18946.2.) As opposed to the tour costs that cannot be pro-rated, allowing pro-rating of other
benefits such as food and beverages is an accurate depiction of the benefit to each official because
the official is the only one receiving the benefit of those types of items and those costs would not
have been incurred had the official not have been present.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

sections.analysis:
The Act requires lobbyist employers to disclose payments made in connection with 
influencing legislative and administrative action. (Section 86115.) Among other items, lobbyist 
employers must itemize activity expenses and payments which would not have been made except to 
support or assist lobbying activity. (Section 86116.) Activity expenses include gifts provided to a 
state official or state candidate. (Section 86111.) 
Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as: 
"(a) "Gift" means, except as provided in subdivision (b), any payment to the 
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is 
made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to 
official status ... " 
"(b) The term "gift" does not include: 
"( l )  Informational material such as books, reports, pamphlets, calendars, or 
periodicals. No payment for travel or reimbursement for any expenses shall be 
deemed "informational material." 
Regulation 18942.1 provides: 
"Informational material" means any goods or service that serves primarily to 
convey infonnation and that is provided to the official for the purpose of assisting 
him or her in the performance of his or her official duties or the duties of the 
elective office he or she seeks. Informational material may include: 
"(c) On-site demonstrations, tours, or inspections. Transportation provided to or 
in connection with an on-site demonstration, tour, or inspection is also considered 
"informational material" when any of the following apply:  
''( 1) The transportation serves as the means by which the infonnation is conveyed 
and is integral to the conveyance of the infonnation, such as an aerial tour over an 
area. 
"(2) The transportation is provided solely at the site of a demonstration, tour, or 
inspection, including to and from an area of that site that is legally inaccessible to 
the public. 
"(3) The transportation is to or from a site when there is no reasonable, publicly-available 
commercial transportation available to that site and the transportation provided is limited to 
the segment for which public transportation is not available." 
Thus, the on-site tour and any transportation that is not commercially obtainable and is also 
an integral part of the on-site tour is considered informational material. 
As noted above, a purpose of these inspection trips is to acquaint elected officials with 
issues facing the water industry and the District's operations, programs, and objectives, both present 
and future. 
Section 86 l l 6(h)( 1) requires lobbyist employers to report "the total of all other payments to 
influence legislative or administrative action . . .. " 
Regulation 18239(d)(4) further provides that '"influencing legislative or administrative 
action ' means communicating directly or taking any other action for the principal purpose of 
supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing any legislative or 
administrative action. '' (Emphasis added.) 
Elected officials are one of the types of individuals targeted for these tours as listed in 
Administrative Code section 261 S(a). Making legislators aware of issues facing the water industry 
and the district 's objectives are ways of influencing potential legislation, especially when 
Legislators are invited on the tours based upon the official positions they hold. Because such 
individuals are selected because they are members of the Legislature (i.e. individuals charged with 
the responsibility of making laws), tours acquainting them with issues facing the water industry and 
with district objectives that in any way attempt to influence legislative or administrative action must 
be disclosed under the category "Other Payments to Influence. " (Regulation 18616.) 
These reported expenses for the bus tour cannot be pro-rated. Section 86 l l 6. 5(a)(2) states 
that "{p]ayments and any other expenses which would not have been incurred but for the filer's 
activities to influence or attempt to influence legislative or administrative action" must be reported. 
Metropolitan must report the entire amount for the bus tour as it currently does, because that is the 
amount of the expenses incurred as a result of the tour and the tour could not have taken place 
without this transportation. Reporting only a portion of the cost of the tour does not reflect an 
accurate picture of the amount expended to influence qualifying officials. Therefore, if qualifying 
officials are on the bus the entire cost of this item must be reported.

However, any transportation which is not part of the on-site tour such as, transportation to 
and from the origin of the tour, are gifts and reportable activity expenses. (Briggs Advice Letter, 
No. A-93-210 and Barker Advice Letter, No. A-93-186.) 
In addition, other benefits, such as food and beverages, are gifts and must be disclosed as 
activity expenses. Because these tours are by invitation only, the pro rata share of the cost of the

and other reportable gifts given to the entire tour group under "Total Amount of Activity", reports 
the total cost of the bus tour under "Other Payments to Influence" on Forms 635 and 640, and 
itemizes all of the expenses for the entire trip over $250 on Form 640. You state that the tours are 
not specifically conducted for or directed at Legislators, though some may attend from time to time.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 30, 2016 Marcia L. Scully General Counsel Metropolitan Water District of Southern California Box 54153 Los Angeles, CA 90054-0153 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-121 Dear Ms. Scully: You are the General Counsel for the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California. This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ on behalf of that organization. Please note that we are only providing advice under the lobbying provisions of the Act and not under other general lobbying prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. QUESTIONS 1. Are the expenses of Metropolitan Water District of California's ("Metropolitan") inspection tours required to be reported on Metropolitan's Lobbyist Employer quarterly report as "Other Payments to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action," when an occasional qualifying reportable state official is a tour guest? 2. Can the total amount of the Activity Expenses for the tours be pro-rated on the report to reflect the total activity costs for only the state qualifying officials present on a tour? 3. If the costs of inspection tours are required to be reported under "Other Payments to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action," can the expenses be pro-rated to reflect only the costs paid for reportable state officials? (For example, if the cost of a tour bus for 40 passengers is $13,000 and there is one qualifying reportable state official on the tour, can the reportable cost for the single qualifying official be calculated on a pro rata basis?) ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-121 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. The expenses of Metropolitan's inspection tours must be reported on Metropolitan's Lobbyist Employer quarterly reports as "Other Payments to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action" when a qualifying reportable state official is a tour guest or when the tours are aimed at influencing state legislative or administrative action.² 2. Yes. The total amount of the Activity Expenses reported can be pro-rated to reflect the total activity costs for only the state qualifying officials present on a tour. 3. If the costs of inspection tours are required to be reported under "Other Payments to Influence Legislative or Administrative Action," and the payment for the transportation is necessary for the tour to take place at all, the reported expenses cannot be pro-rated to reflect the costs paid for only the reportable state officials. The entire cost of the transportation for the tour must be reported regardless of the ratio
